en: Hello and welcome back to Let's Talk. I'm Ananya, your English teacher. English is a
versatile language. It's the language of business and academics. But English can be fun too.
In this lesson, I will cover ten funny short English phrases you would love to use in everyday
conversations. I will explain what they mean and provide examples for everyday use. Hit
like and subscribe to the channel so that you don't miss any interesting lessons like
this one. Ready, let's start.
Here's the first one, hunky dory. Hunky dory, sounds funny, right? Hunky dory means everything
is going just fine and that there are no problems at all. It's a way to say that everything
is okay. Isn't that a great way to say that everything is fine? I'm sure it is. Here are
some examples, after the exam, I felt hunky dory because I knew I did well. My job is
hunky dory and I enjoy working with my team. I was worried about my phone but the technician
said it's hunky dory. Well, now it's your turn to write a few sentences in the comments
with hunky dory and I would love to read your sentences.

Here comes the next one, topsy-turvy. Now if I take this box of batteries, as you can
see these batteries are well arranged, right? What will happen if I turn this box upside
down? They all get mixed up, right? That's what topsy-turvy is. It means everything is
mixed up, messy or not in order. It's a way to say the things are chaotic and confusing.
Let's look at some examples, my schedule has been topsy-turvy since I started my new
job. Moving to a new city made my life topsy-turvy for a while. When you move to a new city,
it takes time to figure out what's where, right? Her plans for the weekend went topsy-turvy
when her friend got sick. So that was topsy-turvy, which means that things are not in order and
I hope that you are making your own sentence and putting them down in the comments.
Here's the next one, razzle-dazzle. Sounds like words from a pop song, right? Razzle-dazzle
means something showy or fancy that is meant to attract attention. Imagine flashy lights
or colours at a party that make it look impressive and entertaining. Something like Yash Raj
films with exotic locations and larger than life screenplay. That's razzle-dazzle. Here
are some examples, the movie had a lot of razzle-dazzle with its special effects and
action scenes. The company used a lot of razzle-dazzle in their advertisements to attract customers.
Team India's victory parade was filled with lots of razzle-dazzle. So in short, razzle-dazzle
means something flashy and exciting. Moving on to the next one, next on my list is, fuddy-duddy.
Now fuddy-duddy is mainly used to describe a person who is old-fashioned or does not
like change or may be boring. They prefer things to stay the same and might not like
the new or modern ideas. I hope you got it. Here are some examples, my grandfather is
a bit of a fuddy-duddy. He still listens to music on vinyl records. The teacher seemed
like a fuddy-duddy because he always talked about how things were better in the past.
He is a fuddy-duddy when it comes to technology. He still uses a flip phone. In short, fuddy-duddy
is someone who is old-fashioned and resists change. I'm pretty sure that was straightforward
to understand. Now the next one is pretty interesting, wishy-washy. Wishy-washy, sounds
like a tagline for a washing machine brand, right? Well, wishy-washy means something that
is not strong. It can be used to describe a person who is not firm on their decisions
or opinions. They keep on changing their mind. In short, it means someone who is indecisive,
unsure and lacking in confidence. It can also be used to describe something that looks like
a scam. Let's look at some example sentences. He gave a wishy-washy answer when I asked
him why he quit his job. She's always wishy-washy when it comes to shopping. His wishy-washy
behaviour during the meeting showed that he wasn't prepared. Alright, that was wishy-washy.
Someone who is unsure or indecisive. Up next, we have bits and bobs, pretty interesting
one. It means small little things or items that are not very important. It's a way to
say a mix of different things that are unimportant. Take a look at these sentences for use in
conversations. I need to organise the bits and bobs on my desk, which means I need to
organise my messy untidy desk. After the trip, I found bits and bobs in my suitcase that
I had forgotten about. The wardrobe is full of bits and bobs that I don't really need.
In short, bits and bobs is a collection of miscellaneous items that are unimportant.
I hope you got it. Keep writing sentences in the comments so that you make a memory
of these wonderful phrases. And we move on to the next one, helter-skelter. Now I use
this word quite often to say something is happening too quickly or in a chaotic way.
It's used to describe a messy or a confused or a chaotic situation. Imagine a fire alarm
going off and people running in all directions. Now that is a helter-skelter situation. It's
a chaotic situation. Let's understand it better with some examples. She searched helter-skelter
for her lost keys. This means she looked everywhere in a frantic way for her missing keys. The
room was helter-skelter after the party. The students left the classroom helter-skelter
when the bell rang. Alright, with that we move on to the next one, nitty-gritty. Now
this word may sound funny but it means the most important or basic parts of something.
It's the essential details that are necessary to understand something. It also means keeping
away all the extra unnecessary stuff and focusing only what truly matters. Here are
some examples. Let's get down to the nitty-gritty and talk about the details of the startup
idea. Before we start, we need to understand the nitty-gritty of how this printer works.
Understanding the nitty-gritty of a contract is important before you sign it. So in short,
nitty-gritty means the most crucial and fundamental parts of something. With that let's move
on to the next one, hocus-pocus. Now this has an interesting meaning. It means words
or actions that are meant to confuse or trick someone. For example, an explanation that
is confusing. You ask a salesman about the phone's battery life. Instead of getting
a direct answer, you get a hocus-pocus explanation. This means the answer was not direct, deceptive
and meaningless and it did not answer the question. Mostly hocus-pocus explanations
or descriptions are used to trick someone. Take a look at these example sentences. Don't
believe all the hocus-pocus about quick money schemes. He dismissed the fortune teller's
predictions as hocus-pocus. The salesperson's pitch seemed like hocus-pocus promising excellent
car mileage. That was too good to be true. This means that he promised a mileage of 50
kilometers per liter of fuel. Now that is hocus-pocus. I hope you got it. With that
here comes the last phrase from today's lesson which is, itsy-bitsy. Something that
is very small or tiny is called itsy-bitsy. It's used to describe something that is
little in size. I'm sure that was easy to understand. Now here are some examples. I
found an itsy-bitsy spider in the corner of my room. I planted some itsy-bitsy seeds
in the garden. He gave me an itsy-bitsy piece of cake. So something that is small and tiny
in size is called itsy-bitsy. And with that we come to the end of today's lesson. I
hope you enjoyed this lesson. I have tried to cover all the nitty-gritties of these short
phrases. Try not to be a fuddy-duddy when it comes to English vocabulary. These new
expressions will certainly razzle-dazzle your conversations. So use them. See I just used
three of them. That's how you practice. I will see you in the next lesson. Until then
keep learning and keep practicing English. Happy learning.
